title: John Williams Is STILL Out Here Making Us Cry With Two Notes And We Have Questions
dek: The man who scored your entire childhood almost said no to Star Wars, faked out Spielberg with a two-note joke that wasn't a joke, and is somehow still working in his 90s — here's the John Williams trivia that's currently breaking the internet.

items.heading: George Lucas Didn't Even Want An Original Score For Star Wars
items.text: Before John Williams ever touched Star Wars, George Lucas planned to just needle-drop classical music over every scene instead of commissioning anything new. Steven Spielberg was the one who insisted Lucas meet Williams, and the rest is, quite literally, movie history.

images.caption: The composer almost never got the Star Wars job at all
Lucas originally wanted classical needle-drops, not an original score

items.heading: He's The Most Oscar-Nominated Living Human On Earth
items.text: John Williams has racked up 54 Academy Award nominations, putting him second all-time behind only Walt Disney's 59. He's also the only person in Oscar history to be nominated across seven consecutive decades.

images.caption: 54 Oscar nominations and counting, second only to Walt Disney

items.heading: Spielberg Genuinely Thought The Jaws Theme Was A Prank
items.text: When Williams sat down at the piano and played the now-iconic "dun dun" for Jaws, Spielberg burst out laughing, convinced his composer was pulling his leg. Williams kept a straight face, insisted he was serious, and delivered one of the most terrifying scores in film history.

images.caption: Spielberg's first reaction to the Jaws theme was straight-up laughter

items.heading: The Whole Shark Was Just Two Notes
items.text: That entire sense of dread in Jaws comes down to a two-note ostinato, a semitone shift from E to F, played over and over by basses and cellos. Williams even had a tuba player perform it uncomfortably high in his range specifically because the strain made it sound more threatening.

images.caption: Two notes. That's it. That's the whole shark.

items.heading: His Partnership With Spielberg Has Now Lasted Over 50 Years
items.text: Williams and Spielberg first teamed up on 1974's The Sugarland Express, and Williams is currently scoring their 30th collaboration together. He's missed only a handful of Spielberg's films in half a century of working together.

images.caption: 50-plus years and 30 films deep with his favorite composer
Just one stop on the longest director-composer partnership in Hollywood

items.heading: You've Heard His Music Every Night On The News And Never Realized It
items.text: In 1985, NBC commissioned Williams to write an entire orchestral suite called "The Mission" for its news broadcasts, and pieces of it are still used on NBC Nightly News today. He said he wanted the music to convey nobility of purpose, consistency, and dignity — for a guy who also wrote the Imperial March, that's a real range flex.

images.caption: Yes, that NBC News fanfare is also a John Williams original

items.heading: He Spent 14 Seasons Running The Boston Pops
items.text: From 1980 to 1993, Williams served as the Boston Pops' principal conductor, taking over from the legendary Arthur Fiedler. He almost quit in 1984 after some orchestra members hissed during a rehearsal of one of his pieces, but he was talked out of resigning and stuck around for nine more years.

images.caption: 14 seasons leading the Boston Pops, hissy rehearsal drama included

items.heading: He's In His 90s And Still Scoring Movies Right Now
items.text: At 93 years old, Williams is currently recording the score for Spielberg's next film, marking their 30th collaboration together. He'd previously hinted at retiring after Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny, but apparently changed his mind — which tracks, since a viral rumor about him retiring "immediately" just made the rounds online and turned out to be false.

images.caption: Still composing, still conducting, still not slowing down at 93
